name: Hina
description: Hina is a powerful and multifaceted goddess in Hawaiian mythology, often associated with the moon, female productivity, kapa making, and healing. She is a primordial deity, appearing in various forms and stories throughout the islands. Some accounts depict her as the wife of the god Kū, while others portray her as a strong, independent woman who leaves her husband to seek a better life. Hina's influence extends to the natural world, influencing tides, weaving, and the growth of plants. She is revered as a creative force and a symbol of female empowerment. One prominent myth describes her journey to the moon to escape the heat of the sun, where she eventually learned to control its power, bringing balance to the world. Her connection to the moon establishes her as a lunar deity, influencing cycles and rhythms. Hina is also a skilled craftswoman, particularly known for her expertise in kapa making, the art of creating cloth from bark. She taught these skills to women, empowering them and contributing to the cultural heritage of Hawaii. Hina's stories are rich with symbolism, representing resilience, resourcefulness, and the importance of balance in the world. She embodies the feminine principle and is a vital figure in Hawaiian spiritual beliefs. Her legends continue to inspire and inform Hawaiian cultural practices, emphasizing the importance of creativity, healing, and respect for the natural world. She is revered as a protector and guide, embodying the strength and wisdom of the feminine divine.
subtitle: Goddess of the Moon and Kapa Making

keyMyths.title: Hina's Journey to the Moon
keyMyths.description: Hina, tired of the sun's heat, travels to the moon. There, she learns to control the moon's power and brings balance to the world.

keyMyths.title: Hina and Kapa Making
keyMyths.description: Hina is credited with inventing kapa making, the art of creating cloth from bark. She taught this skill to women, greatly contributing to Hawaiian culture.

keyMyths.title: Hina and Maui
keyMyths.description: In some stories, Hina is Maui's mother or grandmother. These stories often involve Maui's heroic deeds and Hina's guidance and support.

sources.author: Martha Warren Beckwith
sources.description: A comprehensive compilation of Hawaiian myths and legends.
sources.text: A comprehensive compilation of Hawaiian myths and legends.

sources.author: Rubellite Kawena Johnson
sources.work: Kumulipo: The Hawaiian Hymn of Creation
sources.description: An analysis of the Kumulipo, a Hawaiian creation chant, which mentions Hina.
sources.text: An analysis of the Kumulipo, a Hawaiian creation chant, which mentions Hina.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: God of Wisdom & Writing | Egyptian Mythology

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of kapa cloth, flowers (especially white ones), and food (particularly related to plants and the sea) were traditionally made to Hina at her heiau (temples) or sacred places. These offerings sought her favor for fertility, healing, and success in crafts.

worshipPractices.name: Prayer and Chants
worshipPractices.description: Specific prayers and chants were dedicated to Hina, reciting her lineage, powers, and deeds. These were often performed by kahuna (priests/experts) or by individuals seeking her aid. The chants would invoke her presence and ask for her blessings.

festivals.name: Mahina Hoku (Moon Festival)
festivals.description: While not solely dedicated to Hina, lunar festivals, particularly during the full moon (Mahina Hoku), often honored her as the embodiment of the moon. Celebrations included hula performances, storytelling about Hina's adventures, and feasting.

festivals.name: Kapa Making Rituals
festivals.description: Rituals and prayers were offered to Hina during the kapa making process, as she was considered the patron goddess of this craft. These rituals ensured the quality of the kapa and sought Hina's blessing for the wearer.

cultural.modernLegacy: Hina's legacy continues in modern Hawaiian culture through art, storytelling, and the perpetuation of traditional crafts like kapa making. She is a symbol of female strength, creativity, and connection to the natural world, inspiring artists, writers, and cultural practitioners.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: The origins of Hina are shrouded in the mists of Hawaiian cosmology, reflecting the diverse oral traditions across the islands. In some accounts, she is a primordial goddess, existing from the beginning of time, a manifestation of the feminine creative force. Other narratives portray her as the daughter of Haumea, another powerful earth mother goddess, linking her to the lineage of divine beings responsible for creation. Her birth is often associated with the emergence of the moon, solidifying her connection to the lunar cycles and the rhythms of nature. The variations in her origin stories highlight the fluidity and adaptability of Hawaiian mythology, where different islands and families held unique perspectives on the gods and their roles in the cosmos. Regardless of the specific details, Hina's birth signifies the arrival of a potent feminine force, capable of shaping the world and influencing the lives of mortals.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Hina is a central figure in numerous Hawaiian myths, each showcasing different facets of her character. One prominent story tells of her marriage to the god Kū, a union representing the balance between male and female energies. However, some versions depict their relationship as troubled, leading Hina to leave Kū and seek refuge on the moon, where she became the goddess of the lunar realm. Another tale recounts her journey to Kāne, seeking the secret of fire. Her resourcefulness and determination in obtaining this vital element for humanity demonstrate her role as a benefactor of humankind. Additionally, Hina is celebrated as a skilled kapa maker, using her divine abilities to create beautiful and functional garments. These myths highlight Hina's multifaceted nature: a wife, a runaway, a seeker of knowledge, and a skilled artisan, embodying the diverse roles and strengths of women in Hawaiian society.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Worship of Hina was an integral part of ancient Hawaiian religious practices. She was honored in heiau (temples) dedicated to her, where kahuna (priests) performed rituals and offered prayers. Offerings of kapa cloth, flowers, and food were made to seek her favor for fertility, healing, and success in crafts, particularly kapa making. Specific chants and prayers were dedicated to Hina, reciting her lineage, powers, and deeds. Lunar cycles, especially the full moon, were considered sacred to her, with festivals and celebrations often coinciding with these celestial events. Her worship was deeply intertwined with the natural world, reflecting the Hawaiian reverence for the interconnectedness of all things. While formal temples may no longer exist, the essence of Hina's worship continues through cultural practices, storytelling, and artistic expression, ensuring her enduring presence in Hawaiian spirituality.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Hina's legacy extends far beyond ancient Hawaiian religious practices, continuing to influence contemporary Hawaiian culture and beyond. She remains a powerful symbol of female strength, creativity, and independence. Her stories inspire artists, writers, and cultural practitioners to explore themes of feminine empowerment, connection to nature, and the importance of preserving traditional crafts. The revival of kapa making, in particular, owes much to Hina's association with this art form. Her image and stories appear in contemporary art, literature, and educational materials, ensuring that future generations are aware of her significance. Furthermore, Hina's representation of the moon resonates with modern understandings of lunar cycles and their impact on the environment and human well-being, solidifying her enduring relevance in a rapidly changing world. She serves as a potent reminder of the enduring power of mythology and its ability to shape cultural identity and inspire creativity.

shortDescription: Hina is a major Hawaiian goddess associated with the moon, feminine power, creation, and healing. She appears in diverse forms across Hawaiian myths.
